headline: David Thompson Establishes Saleesh House, 1809
body:
The vast, untamed heart of the continent, a land of towering peaks and rushing waters, held secrets yet to be fully unveiled by the gaze of the European. It was into this magnificent wilderness, on a crisp November day in 1809, that the intrepid David Thompson, a man whose spirit was as boundless as the landscapes he charted, pressed forward. With James McMillan and the sturdy men of the North West Company, he sought to plant a seed of commerce and connection amidst the grandeur of what would become Montana. Near the roaring cascades that would one day bear the name Thompson Falls, a humble yet profoundly significant structure began to rise from the earth: Saleesh House. This was no mere shelter, but a beacon, the very first trading post to pierce the formidable barrier of the Continental Divide in this western expanse. It stood as a testament to human endeavor, a point of convergence where the ancient rhythms of the land met the burgeoning pulse of the fur trade.

Thompson, ever the keen observer, understood the pulse of this wild country. He had, earlier that same year, established Kullyspell House, a precursor, but Saleesh House, nestled strategically along the Clark Fork River—which he, in his meticulous fashion, often referred to as the Saleesh River, after the indigenous Flathead people—proved to be a masterstroke of placement. Its location was a natural nexus, a well-trodden path connecting the high mountain hunting grounds with the rich bison pastures near the shimmering expanse of Flathead Lake. Here, diverse tribes, drawn by the bounty of the land, traditionally wintered, and it was here that the North West Company found a willing and skilled populace, the Salish, ready to trade the prized beaver pelts for European goods. The post swiftly blossomed into a vibrant hub, a testament to the symbiotic dance between nature's abundance and human enterprise. It was during these formative months, in the late autumn of 1809 and the nascent winter of 1810, that the Salish people, recognizing Thompson's profound connection to the celestial sphere, bestowed upon him the evocative moniker of "Koo-koo-Sint," the Star-Looker.

The life of a fur trader on this raw frontier was one of ceaseless challenge, a constant negotiation with the elements and the vast distances. Thompson's own journals, though often terse, reveal the arduous nature of their existence. Years later, upon a return to Saleesh House, he would record the stark reality of frontier life, a testament to the relentless forces of nature and the transient nature of human constructs in the wild. As he penned on November 19th, 1811, after a journey through rain and snow, he arrived to find the post in a state of disrepair: "thank good Providence arrived at the House, found Mousseau & Le Paquin just arrived from wanderillg abt. 5 day in search of the Kootanae Road to McGillivray's River, when getting alarmed they returned here living on horse meat. Mr. F. McDonald & the others are up the River to trade Provision with the Saleesh Indians &c. The House is in a ruinous condition, nothing whatever done to it. Rainy day, quite wet." This simple entry, devoid of embellishment, speaks volumes of the struggles and the sheer tenacity required to carve out a foothold in this magnificent, yet unforgiving, land. Saleesh House, though often a fragile outpost, stood as a symbol of the enduring human spirit in the face of the grand, indifferent wilderness.
